Clarity.justification: The paper is reasonably well-structured with clear sections covering introduction, related work, methodology, experiments, analysis, limitations, and future work. The mathematical formulations in Section 3 are presented clearly. However, several issues reduce clarity: (1) The paper is excessively long, with the limitations and future work sections (Sections 7-8) being disproportionately extensive relative to the actual contributions. (2) The paper reads more like a technical report or project documentation than a research paper—the failure analysis, while honest, dominates the narrative. (3) The 'Performance Score' metric defined as 1/latency is misleading since it does not measure actual task performance (e.g., F1, accuracy). The paper acknowledges this in limitations but still uses it throughout the results section, which is confusing. (4) The attention-based baseline is not truly an attention-based method since it always falls back to static pruning due to implementation issues, yet the paper discusses it as if it were a genuine attention-based approach with 2x overhead—this overhead comes from the failed attempt to extract attention, not from actual attention-based pruning.

Novelty.justification: The proposed framework combines several existing ideas (hierarchical pruning, Gumbel-Softmax for discrete decisions, MAML for meta-learning, policy gradient methods) without demonstrating that the combination yields any benefit. The hierarchical chunk-then-token pruning idea has been explored in prior work. The meta-learning component (MAML) described in Section 3.3 is never actually implemented or evaluated—the paper only tests on a single task. The RL component described in the methodology is also not clearly reflected in the actual training code, which only uses efficiency and coverage losses. Dynamic Context Pruning (Anagnostidis et al., 2023) already proposed learnable token pruning mechanisms. The paper does not demonstrate any novel insight or working method that advances beyond existing approaches.

Soundness.justification: The experimental methodology has critical flaws that undermine the paper's conclusions: (1) The proposed adaptive method completely fails—it prunes ALL tokens (0% retention), which is a catastrophic training failure, not a meaningful experimental result. (2) No task performance metrics (F1, ROUGE, accuracy) are reported. The paper only measures efficiency metrics (retention rate, latency, memory), making it impossible to assess whether any pruning method preserves task performance. (3) The attention baseline always falls back to static pruning (as confirmed by the logs showing 'Attention pruning failed' for all 50 samples), yet the paper reports it as having 2x latency overhead and discusses attention-based pruning limitations at length—this is misleading since the actual attention-based pruning was never executed. (4) The training loss in the code only includes efficiency_loss and coverage_loss—there is no task_loss component, contradicting the methodology in Section 3.3. The RL component and MAML described in the paper are absent from the implementation. (5) The dataset is tiny (140 train, 50 test samples) and contexts are truncated to 2048 tokens, undermining claims about 'long-context processing.' (6) Only 3 training epochs were used, which the paper itself acknowledges is insufficient. (7) The 'memory usage' metric is computed as a simple multiplication of hidden state size by retention rate, not actual GPU memory measurement. (8) The training logs show retention rates around 0.05 during training but 0.0 during evaluation, suggesting a disconnect between training (soft masks) and evaluation (hard thresholding at 0.5).

Significance.justification: The paper's significance is severely limited because: (1) The proposed method does not work—it prunes all tokens, making it useless in practice. (2) The main finding is that static pruning (keeping first/last 25% of tokens) works well, which is already well-known in the literature. (3) No task performance metrics are reported, so even the baseline comparisons provide limited insight. (4) The experiment uses OPT-125M, a very small model, on a tiny subset of NarrativeQA with truncated contexts—the results cannot generalize to practical scenarios. (5) The meta-learning and RL components described in the methodology are not implemented, so the paper cannot claim contributions in those areas. (6) While the paper frames the failure analysis as a contribution, the insights provided (e.g., 'training was too short,' 'loss weights were imbalanced') are obvious and do not require experimentation to identify. The paper essentially documents a failed experiment without providing actionable solutions.

strengths: Honest acknowledgment of the adaptive method's failure, with detailed analysis of potential causes and proposed solutions in Sections 6.3 and 7
Clear mathematical formulation of the hierarchical pruning framework in Section 3, which could serve as a starting point for future work
The code is provided and the experiments are reproducible, with detailed logging that allows verification of results
weaknesses: The proposed method completely fails (0% retention rate), meaning the core contribution of the paper does not work
No task performance metrics (F1, accuracy, ROUGE) are reported—only efficiency metrics are measured, making it impossible to evaluate whether any method preserves useful information
Critical disconnect between methodology and implementation: the RL component, MAML meta-learning, and task loss described in Section 3 are absent from the actual code
The attention baseline always falls back to static pruning due to implementation issues, yet the paper discusses it as a genuine attention-based method with misleading latency analysis

Extremely small experimental scale: 140 training samples, 50 test samples, OPT-125M model, 2048 token truncation, 3 training epochs—insufficient to draw meaningful conclusions about long-context processing
The paper is disproportionately long relative to its actual contributions, with extensive future work and limitations sections that read more like a project proposal than completed research
